nodes.title: Biomaterials Engineering
nodes.description: A materials science and engineering approach is used to explore the structure-function relationships of natural and bio-inspired materials for various engineering applications. The emphasis is on mechanical design and function. The medical applications of biomaterials will be discussed. Prerequisites: ME 2110, BME 3600, or BME 3605. Cross-listed with: ME 3410.
nodes.other_requirements: Cross-listed with ME 3410. Preqs enforced by the system:ME 2110orBME 3600Cross listed withME 3410; Total combined enrollment = 45

nodes.title: BME Capstone Design I
nodes.description: Teams apply industry-standard biodesign and project management processes to design, build, and test a functional prototype that meets their client's requirements. Prerequisite: BME 3600.
nodes.other_requirements: Open to degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Teaching Assistantship
nodes.description: Undergraduate student service as a teaching assistant, usually in an introductory-level course in the discipline, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required; Variable 1-3 credits

nodes.title: Clinical Devices & Instruments
nodes.description: Focuses on the development, design and adaptation of biomedical devices and instruments in exciting active areas of biomedical device development and applications at UVM and the UVM Medical Center (UVMMC). Includes lectures on commercialization and manufacturing. Team-taught by faculty in the Larner College of Medicine and the UVMMC. Credit not awarded for both BME 5800 and BME 3810. Prerequisites: Biomedical Engineering Graduate student or Instructor permission; Content knowledge in ANPS 1190, ANPS 1200, BME 2000, and BME 2050 is assumed.
nodes.other_requirements: Biomedical Engineering Graduate student or Instructor permission; Graduate standing Biomedical Engineering or instructor permission; Content knowledge in ANPS 1190, ANPS 1200, BME 2000, and BME 2050 is assumed.

nodes.title: Ugr Hum Anatomy & Physiology 1
nodes.description: Part I of two-semester course sequence. Structure and function of human body.
nodes.other_requirements: Prereg limited to: BMCS, BIOM, CSD, EMS, EXSC, HSCI, MLS, MRS, NFS, NMT, PE, PHS, PRNU majors only. Degree students only even after major restrictions lifted.

nodes.title: Design 1: Intro to Design
nodes.description: Introduces design methodologies for biomedical engineers in a lecture format with studio-style, project-based learning. Integrates crucial skills required for successful engineering design in healthcare. Covers scientific (e.g., anatomy), engineering fundamentals (e.g., physics), and hands-on prototyping skills (e.g., 3D printing, soldering, electronics prototyping, coding, etc.). Design experiences emphasize critical thinking, interdisciplinary teamwork, and technical communication. Prerequisites: Biomedical Engineering major.
nodes.other_requirements: Biomedical Engineering major.

nodes.title: Deep Learning in BME
nodes.description: Focuses on the application of advanced neural computing to biological data. Students develop the skills to implement and interpret models for (1) genomic sequences, (2) protein folding, and (3) imaging/microscopy applications. Covers the evolution of deep learning architectures (from simpler convolutional neural nets to more modern transformer-based architectures), the coding skills in Python, and the quantitative logic required to handle deep learning problems in a lab setting. Prerequisite: Experience with computer coding is recommended.
nodes.other_requirements: Experience with computer coding is recommended but not required.

nodes.title: Calculus II
nodes.description: Vectors and vector operations. Techniques and applications of integration. Polar coordinates, Taylor polynomials, sequences and series, power series. Credit will not be given for both MATH 1248 and MATH 1242. No credit following receipt of credit for MATH 2248 or above. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1234. C- or better in MATH 021.

nodes.title: Multi-scale Biomedical Systems
nodes.description: Examines tissues to organs as complex systems operating at multiple levels of length and time scale. A particular focus is on how system behavior emerges in non-trivial ways from the ensemble behavior of components at lower levels of scale. Prerequisites: BME 3000 or BME 3175.

nodes.title: Computational Systems Biology
nodes.description: Covers foundational mathematical models in systems biology, and their computational implementation, describing biological processes from the molecular to the ecological scale. Prerequisite: Beginner knowledge of Python programming is assumed. Pre/Co-requisites: BME 3000, BME 3175, or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Beginner knowledge of Python programming is assumed. Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Graduate Independent Research
nodes.description: Graduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion

nodes.title: Master's Thesis Research
nodes.description: Research for the Master's Thesis.

nodes.title: Signals & Systems
nodes.description: Discrete- and continuous-time signals and systems. Input/output descriptions and analysis. Convolution, Fourier analysis, sampling and Laplace transforms. Application to electrical engineering design problems. Prerequisite: MATH 3201. Pre/Co-requisite: EE 2135 recommended.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission recommended. You are expected to know how to program on a computer (in any programming language). In addition, you are expected to know how to solve simple integrals and ordinary differential equations. You must also know complex numbers.

nodes.title: College Honors
nodes.description: Honors studies leading to a thesis.

nodes.title: Design 2: Regulatory & Testing
nodes.description: Introduces the regulatory, technical, and ethical contexts in which biomedical solutions are developed, tested, and approved or licensed for use in the United States, including content related to the regulatory landscape in the U.S. (e.g., regulatory history, Food and Drug Administration, regulatory pathways, design controls), technical engineering requirements (engineering specifications, risk management), testing (verification, validation, animal studies, clinical trials), and ethics. Prerequisite: BME 1605.

nodes.title: BME Capstone Design II
nodes.description: Teams refine their functional prototype from BME Capstone Design 1 and explore approaches for manufacturing at scale, regulatory strategy, clinical strategy, IP strategy, health-economics and reimbursement. Prerequisite: BME 4600.

nodes.title: Clinical Devices & Instruments
nodes.description: Focuses on the development, design and adaptation of biomedical devices and instruments. Team-taught by faculty in the Larner College of Medicine and UVM Medical Center and focuses on exciting active areas of biomedical device development and applications at UVM and the UVM Medical Center. Credit not awarded for both BME 3810 and BME 5800. Prerequisites: ANPS 1190, ANPS 1200; or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission

nodes.title: IS:Medical Imaging
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: Core 1: Biomechanics & Sensing
nodes.description: Studio-style course that fuses lecture with project-based learning and laboratory exercises. Covers force and torque vectors, systems in equilibrium, physical properties of human body segments and biological systems, kinematics and kinetics of particles and rigid bodies, stress and strain of solid materials, circuits and instrumentation. Prerequisites: C- or better in MATH 1248, and PHYS 1500.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1248 and PHYS 1500; Must register for BME 2000 labs L01-L03

nodes.title: Physics for Engineers I
nodes.description: Mechanics including oscillations and waves. With lab. Accompanying optional problem-solving session: PHYS 1510. Prerequisite: MATH 1234 or MATH 1242.
nodes.other_requirements: School of Engineering students only: CEMS-undeclared, BIOM, CE, EE, EENV, EMGT, ME, CS, DS, MATH; Open to Degree and PACE students;

nodes.title: Wearable Sensing
nodes.description: Covers current state-of-the-art in wearable sensors and the biomechanical and physiological phenomena they are being used to measure. Emphasis will be given to applications related to human health and medicine. Prerequisites: EE 3150, BME 3000, BME 3175, or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission

nodes.title: Rehabilitation Engineering
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles.

nodes.title: Ugr Hum Anatomy & Physiology 2
nodes.description: Part II of two-semester course sequence. Structure and function of human body. Prerequisite: ANPS 1190.
nodes.other_requirements: Common hour exams are held 5:30-7:00pm on these Mondays: 2/9, 3/2, and 4/6. BIOM,CSD,DIET,DNFS,EXSC,HSCI,MLS,NFS, PE, PHS, MRS, PRNU; Majors only until Dec. 2nd, Please contact Patrick Mullen (patrick.c.mullen@uvm.edu) with any questions; Class meets 3x a week with online lab comprising 4th credit; Degree students only even after level restrictions removed; PACE students register for ANPS 1200 ZRA; Prereq enforced by the system:ANPS 1190.

nodes.title: IS:Magnetic Resonance Imaging
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: Materials Engineering
nodes.description: Atomic structure, crystalline structure, mechanical properties and testing of materials, phase equilibria, processing of metals, polymers, and ceramics. Prerequisite: ME 1140.

nodes.title: Advanced Biomedical Systems
nodes.description: Uses the study of lung mechanics as a vehicle for teaching a range of mathematical modeling and data analysis methods central to the study of physiological systems. Students will gain a detailed understanding of how the lung works as a mechanical system and various diseases that affect mechanical function. At the same time, they will learn about applications of a range of mathematical and signal processing techniques. Prerequisite: Graduate standing or Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate standing or Instructor permission

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: BME Design 2
nodes.description: Introduction to verification/validation testing. Case studies of BME Capstone Design II teams. Prerequisite: BME 2600.
nodes.other_requirements: BIOM, EMGT, ENGR majors only

nodes.title: Eng Proteins forDiagno&Therapy
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles.

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Human Cell Biology
nodes.description: Lecture and laboratory experiences about molecular and cellular structure, function and physiology using human cells as the model.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for Lab A01, A02, or A03; students only or Instructor permission; Student Lab Fee: $65.00; Degree students only, PACE students register for BHSC 1340 ZRA

nodes.title: Doctoral Dissertation Research
nodes.description: Research for the Doctoral Dissertation.

nodes.title: Core 2: Materials & Transport
nodes.description: Studio-style course that fuses lecture with project-based learning and laboratory exercises. Covers materials related to medical devices, the biological reaction to implanted medical devices, and associated failure mechanisms. Diffusive and convective mass transport in biochemical interactions, oxygen transport, cell adhesion/signaling, drug and macromolecule transport. Prerequisites: BME 2000, BHSC 1340.
nodes.other_requirements: Exam held 5:30p - 7:00p on 9/30, 10/21, 11/18. Students are expected to read course materials, ask questions during class, participate in in-class activities such as projects and lab experiments as well as complete the class homework and projects. A 6-credit course requires 6 hours of in-class work plus 12-15 hours of work outside of class each week.
